content:
# Luke Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 91-93 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_006_page_001.jpg, LUK_006_page_002.jpg, LUK_006_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass on a sabbath that he was passing through grain fields, and his disciples were plucking the heads of grain and eating, rubbing them in their hands.

**2** And certain of the Pharisees said, "Why do you do what is not lawful on the sabbath?"

**3** And Jesus answered and said to them, "Have you not read even this, what David did when he was hungry, he and those who were with him —

**4** how he entered into the house of God and took the bread of the Presence and ate, and gave to those with him, which is not lawful to eat except for the priests alone?"

**5** And he said to them, "The Son of Man is lord of the sabbath."

**6** And it came to pass on another sabbath that he entered into the synagogue and taught. And there was a man there, and his right hand was withered.

**7** And the scribes and the Pharisees were watching him closely, whether he would heal on the sabbath, that they might find an accusation against him.

**8** But he knew their reasonings, and he said to the man having the withered hand, "Rise and stand in the midst." And he rose and stood.

**9** And Jesus said to them, "I ask you whether it is lawful on the sabbath to do good or to do evil, to save a life or to destroy it?"

**10** And having looked around at them all, he said to him, "Stretch out your hand." And he did so, and his hand was restored.

**11** But they were filled with madness and discussed with one another what they might do to Jesus.

**12** And it came to pass in those days that he went out to the mountain to pray, and he spent the whole night in prayer to God.

**13** And when day came, he called his disciples and chose from them twelve, whom he also named apostles:

**14** Simon, whom he also named Peter, and Andrew his brother, and James and John, and Philip and Bartholomew,

**15** and Matthew and Thomas, and James the son of Alphaeus, and Simon who was called the Zealot,

**16** and Judas the son of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a traitor.

**17** And having come down with them, he stood on a level place, and a great crowd of his disciples, and a great multitude of the people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the coastal region of Tyre and Sidon,

**18** who came to hear him and to be healed from their diseases; and those being troubled by unclean spirits were healed.

**19** And all the crowd was seeking to touch him, because power was going out from him and healing all.

**20** And he, having lifted up his eyes upon his disciples, said: "Blessed are the poor, for yours is the kingdom of God.

**21** Blessed are those who hunger now, for you shall be satisfied. Blessed are those who weep now, for you shall laugh.

**22** Blessed are you when men hate you, and when they exclude you and reproach you and cast out your name as evil on account of the Son of Man.

**23** Rejoice in that day and leap for joy, for behold, your reward is great in heaven; for in the same manner their fathers did to the prophets.

**24** But woe to you who are rich, for you have received your consolation.

**25** Woe to you who are filled now, for you shall hunger. Woe to you who laugh now, for you shall mourn and weep.

**26** Woe to you when all men speak well of you, for in the same manner their fathers did to the false prophets.

**27** But I say to you who hear: Love your enemies, do good to those who hate you,

**28** bless those who curse you, pray for those who mistreat you.

**29** To the one who strikes you on the cheek, offer the other also; and from the one who takes your cloak, do not withhold your tunic either.

**30** Give to everyone who asks of you, and from the one who takes what is yours, do not ask it back.

**31** And just as you wish that men should do to you, do to them likewise.

**32** And if you love those who love you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners love those who love them.

**33** And if you do good to those who do good to you, what credit is that to you? For even sinners do the same.

**34** And if you lend to those from whom you hope to receive, what credit is that to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, that they may receive back the equal amount.

**35** But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, expecting nothing in return; and your reward shall be great, and you shall be sons of the Most High, for he is kind to the ungrateful and evil.

**36** Be merciful, just as your Father is merciful.

**37** And do not judge, and you shall not be judged; and do not condemn, and you shall not be condemned. Forgive, and you shall be forgiven.

**38** Give, and it shall be given to you: good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over, they shall give into your lap. For with the measure you measure, it shall be measured back to you."

**39** And he spoke a parable to them: "Is a blind man able to lead a blind man? Will they not both fall into a pit?

**40** A disciple is not above his teacher, but everyone who is fully trained shall be as his teacher.

**41** And why do you look at the speck that is in the eye of your brother, but the beam that is in your own eye you do not consider?

**42** How are you able to say to your brother, 'Brother, let me take out the speck that is in your eye,' when you yourself do not see the beam in your own eye? Hypocrite, first take out the beam from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to take out the speck that is in the eye of your brother.

**43** For there is no good tree that produces rotten fruit, nor again a rotten tree that produces good fruit.

**44** For each tree is known by its own fruit. For figs are not gathered from thorns, nor are grapes harvested from a bramble bush.

**45** The good man out of the good treasure of his heart brings forth the good, and the evil man out of the evil treasure brings forth the evil; for out of the abundance of the heart his mouth speaks.

**46** And why do you call me 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?

**47** Everyone who comes to me and hears my words and does them — I will show you what he is like:

**48** He is like a man building a house, who dug and deepened and laid a foundation upon the rock. And when a flood came, the river burst against that house and was not able to shake it, because it had been well built.

**49** But the one who heard and did not do is like a man who built a house upon the earth without a foundation, against which the river burst, and immediately it collapsed, and the ruin of that house was great."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **Page layout:** Luke 6 begins on page 91 (folio 46v), column 3, immediately following the conclusion of Luke 5:39. The chapter continues through page 92 (folio 47r) across all four columns and concludes on page 93 (folio 47v), columns 1-2, where Luke 7 begins.

2. **Verse 1 — "on a sabbath" (EN SABBATW):** Sinaiticus reads EN SABBATW. Some manuscripts read EN SABBATW DEUTEROPRWTW ("on the second-first sabbath"), a notoriously obscure expression. Sinaiticus omits DEUTEROPRWTW entirely, and the LOGOS follows the manuscript as written. The simpler reading is widely regarded as original; DEUTEROPRWTW appears to be a later scribal gloss.

3. **Verse 1 — "rubbing them in their hands" (YWXONTEC TAIC XERCIN):** This detail is unique to Luke among the Synoptics. The participle YWXONTEC ("rubbing") specifies the action that the Pharisees construe as threshing, and thus as work forbidden on the sabbath. The LOGOS preserves the participial construction.

4. **Verse 5 — "The Son of Man is lord of the sabbath":** Sinaiticus places this declaration at verse 5, following the David precedent. The LOGOS preserves this positioning. Some Western manuscripts (notably Codex D) insert after verse 4 an additional logion about a man working on the sabbath, but Sinaiticus contains no such addition.

5. **Verse 6 — "on another sabbath" (EN ETERW SABBATW):** Sinaiticus reads EN ETERW SABBATW, clearly distinguishing this incident from the grain-field episode. The LOGOS renders ETERW as "another," preserving the temporal distinction Luke draws between the two sabbath controversies.

6. **Verse 6 — "his right hand" (H XEIR AUTOU H DEXIA):** Luke alone among the Synoptics specifies that it was the right hand, a detail that may reflect medical precision or an awareness of the greater social and practical consequence of disability in the dominant hand. The LOGOS preserves this specificity.

7. **Verse 10 — "was restored" (APEKATESTAQH):** Sinaiticus reads APEKATESTAQH. Some manuscripts add UGIHC WC H ALLH ("healthy as the other"), but Sinaiticus lacks this expansion. The LOGOS follows the shorter reading of Sinaiticus.

8. **Verse 11 — "madness" (ANOIAC):** Sinaiticus reads ANOIAC, literally "mindlessness" or "folly," rendered "madness" to convey the intensity of irrational fury that Luke describes. This is stronger than mere annoyance; Luke portrays the opponents as having lost rational composure.

9. **Verse 13 — "apostles" (APOSTOLOUC):** Sinaiticus reads APOSTOLOUC. Luke is the only Synoptic evangelist to use the term APOSTOLOC in the selection narrative itself. The word denotes "sent ones" — commissioned envoys. The LOGOS retains the transliterated form "apostles," now a standard English word.

10. **Verse 15 — "the Zealot" (TON KALOUMENON ZHLWTHN):** Sinaiticus reads TON KALOUMENON ZHLWTHN ("who was called the Zealot"). The designation ZHLWTHC may indicate membership in a revolutionary movement or simply denote religious zeal. The LOGOS renders it "the Zealot" as a proper designation, following the manuscript.

16. **Verse 16 — "Judas Iscariot" (IOUDAN ISKARIWTHN):** Sinaiticus reads ISKARIWTHN. The LOGOS retains the traditional transliteration. The epithet likely derives from Hebrew ISH QERIYYOT ("man of Kerioth"), identifying Judas's place of origin.

11. **Verse 17 — "a level place" (TOPOU PEDINOU):** Sinaiticus reads EPI TOPOU PEDINOU ("on a level place"). PEDINOC means "flat, level," distinguishing this setting from Matthew's "mountain" (Matt 5:1). The LOGOS preserves Luke's distinct topographical note without harmonizing with Matthew.

12. **Verse 20 — "Blessed are the poor" (MAKARIOI OI PTWXOI):** Sinaiticus reads OI PTWXOI without the Matthean qualifier TW PNEUMATI ("in spirit," Matt 5:3). Luke's version addresses material poverty directly. The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus exactly, without supplementing from Matthew.

13. **Verse 24-26 — The Woes:** Luke alone among the evangelists records these four woes as counterparts to the four beatitudes. Sinaiticus preserves all four. The LOGOS translates OUAI as "Woe," maintaining the prophetic tone of denunciation inherited from the Old Testament prophetic tradition (cf. Isa 5:8-23).

14. **Verse 29 — "cloak...tunic" (IMATION...XITWNA):** In Luke's order, the outer garment (IMATION, "cloak") is taken first, then the inner garment (XITWN, "tunic") is not to be withheld. This reverses Matthew's order (Matt 5:40), where the legal scenario of seizing the tunic comes first. The LOGOS preserves Luke's sequence without harmonization.

15. **Verse 35 — "expecting nothing in return" (MHDEN APELPIZONTEC):** Sinaiticus reads MHDEN APELPIZONTEC. The verb APELPIZW is rare, occurring only here in the New Testament. Its precise sense is debated: "expecting nothing back" or "despairing of nothing." The LOGOS renders "expecting nothing in return," which fits the context of lending without reciprocity.

16. **Verse 38 — "into your lap" (EIC TON KOLPON UMWN):** The KOLPOC refers to the fold of the garment above the belt, used as a pocket or pouch for carrying goods. The LOGOS renders "into your lap" as a formal equivalent that preserves the image of generous, overflowing measure.

17. **Verse 40 — "fully trained" (KATHRTISMENOC):** Sinaiticus reads KATHRTISMENOC, from KATARTIZW, which means "to make complete, to restore, to fully train." The LOGOS renders "fully trained" to capture the pedagogical context: the disciple's goal is to attain the level of the teacher, not to surpass him.

18. **Verse 48 — "dug and deepened" (ESKACEN KAI EBAQUNEN):** Sinaiticus reads ESKACEN KAI EBAQUNEN, two verbs emphasizing the thoroughness of the builder's preparation. Luke's version is more elaborate than Matthew's parallel (Matt 7:24-25). The LOGOS preserves both verbs, reflecting the deliberate effort of the wise builder.

19. **Verse 48 — "because it had been well built" (DIA TO KALWC OIKODOMHCQAI AUTHN):** Sinaiticus reads this phrase rather than the variant TEQEMELIWTO GAR EPI THN PETRAN ("for it had been founded upon the rock") found in other manuscripts. The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus, which attributes the house's stability to the quality of its construction rather than restating the foundation.

20. **Verse 49 — "immediately it collapsed" (EUQUC SUNEPESEN):** Sinaiticus reads EUQUC ("immediately"), underscoring the sudden and catastrophic nature of the collapse. The LOGOS preserves this adverb, which heightens the contrast between the two builders.

### Translation Philosophy

- **"Heads of grain" (STAXUAC):** Rendered "heads of grain" (v. 1) rather than the archaic "ears of corn." STAXUC denotes the spike or head of a cereal plant, and "grain" avoids the British English ambiguity of "corn" (which in American English refers specifically to maize).
- **"Bread of the Presence" (ARTOUC THC PROQESEWC):** Rendered "bread of the Presence" (v. 4) rather than "showbread" (KJV). The Greek PROQESIC ("setting forth") translates the Hebrew lehem happanim ("bread of the face/presence"), referring to the twelve loaves set before the LORD in the tabernacle (Lev 24:5-9). "Bread of the Presence" preserves the theological meaning.
- **"Credit" (XARIC):** Rendered "credit" (vv. 32-34) rather than "thanks" or "grace." In this context, XARIC denotes the merit or benefit one gains — "what credit is that to you?" captures the rhetorical force of Jesus's argument that loving only those who reciprocate earns no distinction.
- **"Sons of the Most High" (UIOI UYISTOU):** Retained as a literal rendering (v. 35) of the Semitic idiom expressing likeness to or derivation from a quality. "Sons of the Most High" conveys both the relational and the ethical dimension: those who act mercifully reveal their kinship with the merciful God.
- **"Speck...beam" (KARFOC...DOKON):** Rendered "speck" and "beam" (vv. 41-42) to preserve the hyperbolic contrast between the tiny splinter (KARFOC) and the massive timber (DOKOC). The absurdity of the image is essential to Jesus's rhetorical force.
- **"Bramble bush" (BATOU):** Rendered "bramble bush" (v. 44) for BATOC, which denotes a thorny, wild shrub. This distinguishes it from AKANQWN ("thorns") in the same verse, preserving Luke's use of two distinct botanical terms.
